facts_disclosed: Maria retired, new housekeeper Ana (Maria's niece)

turns.content: What is the best way to get hard water stains off of glass shower doors? I have tried vinegar and it barely made a difference.

Vinegar alone often isn't strong enough for heavy hard water buildup, especially in areas like South Florida where the water is very mineral-rich. A few options that work better:

1. **Vinegar + baking soda paste** — Make a thick paste, apply it to the glass, let it sit for 30 minutes, then scrub with a non-scratch sponge. The abrasion of the baking soda helps where vinegar alone can't.

2. **Bar Keepers Friend** — The powdered version. Make a paste with water, apply with a damp cloth, let it sit 10 minutes, then wipe. This is probably the most effective over-the-counter option for mineral deposits.

3. **Magic Eraser (melamine sponge)** — Surprisingly effective on hard water stains on glass. Wet it and scrub in circular motions.

4. **CLR (Calcium Lime Rust)** — For severe buildup. Spray it on, wait five minutes, scrub, rinse thoroughly. Use in a ventilated bathroom.

How bad is the buildup? Are we talking a light haze or thick white mineral deposits?

It is more than a haze. It has been building up for a while and I am only now dealing with it because we have a new housekeeper and she asked me what products I wanted her to use. Our old housekeeper Maria always handled everything herself — she had her own system, her own products, she knew exactly what to do. But Maria retired last month, Dios mio, and now her niece Ana has taken over.

Ah, that's a big transition — losing someone who ran the house on autopilot for a long time. It makes sense that things like the shower doors would surface now, since the institutional knowledge walked out the door with Maria.

For Ana, I'd recommend setting her up with these basics for the bathrooms:

- **Bar Keepers Friend** for hard water on glass and fixtures
- **Scrubbing Bubbles** or similar daily shower spray to prevent future buildup between deep cleans
- **A squeegee** by the shower — if anyone in the house can squeegee the glass after each use, it prevents 80% of hard water staining from forming

For the existing heavy buildup, she might need to do two or three rounds with Bar Keepers Friend before the glass is fully clear. It takes patience — each round gets a layer off.

How is the transition going otherwise? Is Ana picking things up quickly?

She is. She is a different style than Maria — a little slower, more methodical. Maria would blow through the house like a hurricane and everything would just be done. Ana takes her time, but I will say this: she is thorough. She cleans things Maria never touched. She moved the living room furniture last week and cleaned behind the couch. I did not even know there was dust behind the couch.

That's actually a great sign. Speed comes with familiarity — once Ana learns the layout and rhythm of your house, she'll get faster naturally. But thoroughness is a disposition, not a learned skill. You can teach someone to be faster; you can't easily teach someone to care about what's behind the couch.

It sounds like Maria trained her well on the fundamentals, even if their approaches are different. Did Maria specifically send Ana your way, or did you find her separately?

Maria sent her. She said, 'Senora Rosa, I am retiring but I am not going to leave you with nobody. My niece Ana needs the work and she is a good girl. I trained her myself.' And that was that. Maria does not ask — she arranges. I have always respected that about her.

Maria sounds like a woman who takes ownership of her responsibilities down to the last detail — including the succession plan. That kind of loyalty and forethought after years of working together says a lot about both of you.

And having Ana come in pre-trained by Maria, with knowledge of your standards and expectations, is infinitely better than hiring a stranger. There's a built-in trust through the family connection. It takes one of the hardest parts of the transition off the table.

For the products question Ana asked about — would you like me to put together a simple cleaning supply list organized by room? That way she has a reference and you don't have to field questions every week as new situations come up.

Yes, actually, that would be very helpful. Keep it simple — kitchen, bathrooms, floors, and general surfaces. Nothing fancy. Maria used to bring her own products but Ana does not have that system yet, so I told her I would supply everything.

Here's a straightforward list:

**Kitchen:**
- Dawn dish soap (the original blue — cuts grease better than anything)
- Bar Keepers Friend powder (stainless steel, sinks, stovetop)
- Clorox disinfecting wipes (quick counter wipedowns)
- White vinegar in a spray bottle (glass, microwave interior)

**Bathrooms:**
- Bar Keepers Friend (shower glass, faucets, hard water)
- Scrubbing Bubbles daily shower spray (prevention between deep cleans)
- Clorox toilet bowl cleaner with the angled neck
- Microfiber cloths for mirrors and chrome

**Floors:**
- Swiffer WetJet or a good mop with Fabuloso (the lavender one is the most popular for a reason)
- Broom and dustpan for the kitchen tile

**General surfaces:**
- Pledge or Murphy's Oil Soap for wood furniture
- Microfiber cloths — buy a big pack, they're reusable and lint-free
- A duster with an extendable handle for ceiling fans and high shelves

That covers about 95% of household cleaning. Ana can build her own system from there as she learns the house.
Fabuloso. Claro, of course. Maria used Fabuloso too — the lavender. I think that is non-negotiable in any Cuban or Peruvian household. The house does not smell clean unless it smells like Fabuloso.

That is an ironclad cultural truth. Fabuloso lavender is the standard by which cleanliness is measured in any self-respecting Latin household. The scent alone communicates that the floors have been properly mopped and the house is in order. No substitutes accepted.

Ana will probably appreciate having that continuity from Maria's approach. Some things should not change in a transition, and Fabuloso is one of them.

Now, one more thing. Ana asked me about the schedule — how often, what days. Maria came twice a week, Tuesdays and Fridays. Is that still the right frequency, or should I adjust anything for the transition period?

Twice a week is a good standard frequency for a household your size. Tuesdays and Fridays specifically give you a nice rhythm — one visit early in the week to reset from the weekend, and one at the end to send you into the weekend with a clean house.

During the transition, you might consider a temporary third day — say a Wednesday or Thursday for the first month or two — just so Ana can get through some of those deeper tasks that Maria had on an unspoken rotation: baseboards, inside the oven, window tracks, that kind of thing. Once she's caught up and has her own rhythm, you can drop back to two days.

The alternative is keeping it at two but telling Ana that one of those days each week should include one deep-clean task in addition to the regular routine. That way she works through the house systematically without adding a third visit.

I like the second option. Two days a week, one deep-clean task per visit on a rotation. That is structured and manageable. Maria never needed a rotation — she just knew — but Ana is learning the house, so having it written down will help her.

Exactly. A simple rotation might look like this:

- **Week 1, Tuesday:** Deep clean kitchen (oven, behind appliances, inside fridge)
- **Week 1, Friday:** Deep clean master bathroom (grout, shower doors, under-sink cabinets)
- **Week 2, Tuesday:** Deep clean floors (move furniture, baseboards, under rugs)
- **Week 2, Friday:** Deep clean guest areas (windows, ceiling fans, light fixtures)

Then repeat. After a couple of months she'll have internalized it and won't need the list anymore — just like Maria. The goal is to give Ana the scaffolding so she can eventually operate on instinct the way Maria did.
Scaffolding. Yes, that is the right word. I will type this up and leave it for her. Gracias — you have made this much less overwhelming. I was dreading having to figure all of this out myself.

You shouldn't have to figure it out yourself — Maria handled it for so long that there was no reason for you to think about cleaning logistics. Now you have a supply list, a schedule, and a deep-clean rotation. That's a complete system for Ana to step into.

And give yourself credit for managing this transition well. Losing someone who ran your household is genuinely disruptive, and you're setting Ana up to succeed rather than just hoping she figures it out. That's good management — on the bench or at home.

You are absolutely right. I am going to order everything on this list this weekend. And the squeegee for the shower — I like that idea. If I can get Carlos to actually use it, that will solve half the problem. Thank you.

You're welcome. Getting Carlos to squeegee may be the harder challenge — but if anyone can issue that directive with authority, I suspect it's you. Good luck with the shower doors, and I think Ana is going to work out just fine. The fact that she's already asking the right questions is a very good sign.
